artifacts.content:
Hi Dan,

I’m hiding in a conference room between meetings and trying to sort out the next couple of weeks in my head, so I’m writing it down for both of us.

Work first: I’ve got the monthly confidential mail run coming up again on the 5th business day, and I’m already twitchy after what happened with that Feb 5 test batch at 2 N. Meridian. When the vendor’s inserter misaligned the address windows and those full Social Security numbers and birthdates were briefly visible on a few envelopes before we halted the run, I honestly felt sick. I sent Karen that “Potential PHI/SSN exposure – Feb 5 test batch – need to discuss before reporting” email and we’ve tightened things since, but it’s still in the back of my mind every time I send a file over SFTP.

On top of that, the budget office is breathing down our necks. After the March 12 budget meeting, my director basically said if we can’t negotiate lower print-and-mail costs by next fiscal year, Vital Records Ops could be first in line for FTE cuts. I hate knowing my unit is on the chopping block and not being able to tell the team anything concrete.

Which is why I keep thinking about our own money. I know we agreed in January to pause Ellie’s 529 contributions and float that ~$4,000 on the Chase Visa for a while because of marching band fees and groceries, but it still makes me anxious. If this contract doesn’t renew and I lose the little overtime bump I get from shepherding these monthly mailings, paying that down and restarting the 529 is going to be harder.

Meanwhile, I’m trying to protect my Tuesdays at Midtown with Dr. O’Donnell. I told the vendor I’m not available after 4:00 on Tuesdays because of a standing Midtown Health appointment for stress management, and I’m not giving that up. The insomnia was getting bad again before I went back to biweekly sessions at Eskenazi Midtown last fall.

Can we look at the calendar tonight after the kids are in bed and figure out who’s doing pickups next week around all this? I also want to make sure we leave time to get up to Carmel to see Mom.

– M

Hi Steven,

Catching up on a few things before I head out for Jake’s tutoring, so I wanted to circle back on the contract language you sent.

On duration: I’m hesitant to lean too hard on the “expected to continue indefinitely” phrasing for the monthly mailings. After what [director] said in the March 12 budget discussion about cutting FTEs in our shop first if we can’t get the vendor’s pricing down by next fiscal year, I don’t want to overpromise. You and I both know this program is stable in terms of workload, but the budget office is looking at Vital Records and Health Statistics as a place to trim operations.

On data handling: I’d like to keep the “no samples retained unless explicitly requested in writing” clause exactly as-is, maybe even bold it. After what Michael Turner asked for last October for his “training deck” — those sample letters with real data, even though he claimed they’d be redacted — I’m not comfortable with anything leaving the building that isn’t strictly necessary. The fact that he joked in that meeting at 2 N. Meridian, “You trust us with half the state’s birth records anyway,” is still stuck in my head. I did flag that informally with HR and started a little “Turner – boundary concerns” file just in case, and I’d rather our contract language give us cover to say no.

Also, I keep thinking back to that Feb 5 test run where the misaligned window briefly exposed SSNs and DOBs before we stopped the equipment. Even though we caught it fast, that’s exactly why I want them shredding overruns and spoiled pieces on-site the same business day, cross-cut, no exceptions.

If you’re okay with those tweaks, I’ll run the redline past Karen tomorrow morning.

Thanks,
Melissa
Program Manager, Vital Records Operations
Indiana Department of Health

metadata.subject: Re: Draft language for print/mail contract renewal

artifacts.content:
Hi Susan,

Finally sitting down after Milo’s walk and thought I’d actually answer your last message instead of just liking it and forgetting.

We’re…okay. Tired. Money has been tighter than I like to admit. In January, after I sat down with the spreadsheet and looked at what marching band is going to cost if Ellie does it at North Central (fees, uniforms, buses every weekend), plus what we’re already spending on groceries, Dan and I decided to pause contributions to her 529. We’ve also been carrying about $4,000 on the Chase Visa month-to-month — first time since grad school I’ve had to float debt like that. I told him that out loud and then immediately wanted to crawl under a rock.

Part of why I’m so focused on getting this print-and-mail contract renewed for multiple years is selfish: if it fell apart and my overtime for the monthly mailings disappeared, paying down that balance and restarting the 529 would be a lot harder. And at work, there’s this added stress that our division might be where they cut FTEs if the vendor won’t drop their pricing. Not exactly a soothing backdrop.

On the family front, Mom has me more worried than she’ll admit. You remember when she fell at her condo in Carmel back in November 2024? She ended up at IU Health North ER, and the doctor there mentioned “early signs of mild cognitive impairment” in his notes. Since January I’ve basically been managing her bills and meds behind the scenes. My brother Mark and I have started talking about power of attorney if her memory keeps slipping, but we haven’t said the words to her yet.

Meanwhile Ellie is 13 and living on her phone, and Jake is Jake — sweet, distractible, and perpetually sticky. We’re taking him to math tutoring at the Michigan Road library twice a week, which he tolerates only because I let him pick a graphic novel afterward.

How are you holding up with your shifts? Next time you’re up this way, let’s do coffee without kids so I can hear about your world too.

Love,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Catching up for real this time

artifacts.content:
Hi Ms. Reynolds,

Thank you for reaching out about Ellie. I really appreciate you taking the time to let us know what you’re seeing at Westlane.

Your email about the “Concern re: Ellie – anxiety around grades and social media” honestly didn’t surprise me, but it did make my stomach drop a little. She’s mentioned trouble sleeping the night before big tests, and I’ve noticed she gets panicky if she thinks a friend is mad at her because of something on Instagram or Snapchat. The fact that she asked to see you twice in one week tells me she’s feeling it more than she lets on at home.

At the same time, we’ve had a lot going on in the background. My mom (Ellie’s grandma) fell at her condo in Carmel in November 2024 and ended up at IU Health North. The ER physician there noted some early signs of mild cognitive impairment, and since January I’ve been informally managing her bills and medications, plus talking with my brother Mark about the possibility of pursuing power of attorney if things get worse. I’m sure some of that stress bleeds into the house, even when we think we’re shielding the kids.

I also work full-time downtown at the Indiana Department of Health, in Vital Records, which has been particularly stressful this year due to some data-security issues and budget pressures. I’ve been seeing a therapist, Dr. Karen O’Donnell at Eskenazi Midtown Mental Health, for work-related stress and insomnia since 2023. I’m sharing that just to say we take mental health seriously in this house, and I want Ellie to feel the same way about asking for help that I do now.

Could we set up a time to meet, maybe next Thursday after school, to talk about strategies for her test anxiety and social media boundaries? I’m happy to come in person.

Thank you again for looking out for her.

Best,
Melissa Carpenter
(Ellie’s mom)

metadata.subject: Re: Concern re: Ellie – anxiety around grades and social media

artifacts.content:
Thursday to-do / brain dump – 2 N. Meridian, Suite 109

WORK
- Prep March monthly mailing file for vendor – double-check that SSNs are masked wherever possible. I keep thinking about that Feb 5, 2025 test batch where the misaligned address window briefly exposed full SSNs and birthdates on a few envelopes before we stopped the run. I never want to write another “Potential PHI/SSN exposure – Feb 5 test batch – need to discuss before reporting” email to Karen.
- Call vendor PM (NOT Michael Turner). Still irritated about his October 2024 “training deck” request for sample vital records letters with real data and his “You trust us with half the state’s birth records anyway” joke in the conference room here. Remember I have that “Turner – boundary concerns” file from when I talked to HR.
- Review Steven’s redlines on contract renewal. Keep language about: monthly mailings around 5th business day; SFTP encrypted CSV/PDF; cross-cut shredding of overruns/misprints same business day; no samples retained unless explicitly requested by IDOH program management.
- Budget stress: director said in the March 12 meeting that if we can’t get print/mail pricing down, Vital Records Ops could see FTE reductions next fiscal year. Need to start contingency planning, even if I can’t tell the team yet.

FAMILY
- Call IU Health North clinic back re: Mom’s follow-up on the mild cognitive impairment note from her November 2024 fall. Ask about timing for repeat cognitive testing.
- Talk to Mark this weekend about next steps on possible power of attorney if Mom’s memory keeps slipping.
- Respond to Ellie’s counselor about the “Concern re: Ellie – anxiety around grades and social media” email; confirm meeting time.
- Jake – pick him up for math tutoring at Michigan Road library at 5:30; bring his clarinet so we can go straight to band afterward.

SELF
- Midtown – Dr. O’Donnell – burnout check-in, Tuesday 4:00 p.m., Eskenazi Health Midtown Mental Health. Protect that time.
- Try to get to bed by 10:30. No doomscrolling.

Event: Midtown – Dr. O’Donnell – burnout check-in
Date: Tuesday, March 25, 2025
Time: 4:00–4:50 p.m.
Location: Eskenazi Health Midtown Mental Health, outpatient clinic
Attendees: Melissa Carpenter; Dr. Karen O’Donnell

Description/Notes:
Biweekly therapy session to keep a handle on work-related stress and insomnia. Started seeing Dr. O’Donnell at Midtown back in August 2023 when the Vital Records workload and constant “don’t screw up the SSNs” pressure started bleeding into everything. Lately it’s not just the day-to-day, it’s the bigger stuff: the February 5 test batch at 2 N. Meridian where the misaligned address window briefly exposed full Social Security numbers and birthdates before we halted the run; the March 12 budget meeting where director floated cutting FTEs in my unit if we can’t get the print-and-mail vendor’s pricing down; Michael Turner’s October 2024 “training deck” request and his “You trust us with half the state’s birth records anyway” joke that I ended up reporting informally to HR and documenting in that “Turner – boundary concerns” file.

Also need to talk about home stress: Mom’s November 2024 fall at her Carmel condo and the IU Health North ER note about early mild cognitive impairment; managing her bills and meds since January 2025; ongoing conversations with Mark about possible power of attorney. Plus Ellie’s anxiety around grades and social media – the school counselor’s early March email (“Concern re: Ellie – anxiety around grades and social media”) and our upcoming meeting.

Remind myself: this is protected time. Decline late-afternoon vendor calls on Tuesdays with “standing Midtown Health appointment for stress management.”

Thread: Melissa / coworker (Lena) – rides & rescheduling

Lena: You okay to drive the boxes to the post office again on Friday?

Melissa: Honestly I’d rather not. After getting dragged into that small-claims mess with the school pickup accident and the Collins lawsuit, I’m weird about driving big loads for work.

Lena: Oh wow, I didn’t know there was a lawsuit.

Melissa: Yeah, back in Sept 2022 at Greenbriar pickup. Little fender-bender, other mom (Jennifer Collins) claimed neck injuries. “Collins v. Carpenter” in Marion County Small Claims. My insurer settled for a modest amount early 2023 and the case got dismissed, but I still tense up in school zones.

Lena: Yikes. Okay, say no more. I’ll see if courier services can grab the mail tubs.

Melissa: Thank you. Between that and everything else, my nerves are shot.

Lena: Everything else = budget drama?

Melissa: Budget + vendor drama + home stuff. Director basically said in the March 12 meeting that if we can’t get print/mail costs down, our unit might lose FTEs. And I’m still replaying that Feb 5 test batch where the misaligned address window exposed SSNs/DOBs for a minute before we stopped the run. Not my favorite combo.

Lena: Ugh. No wonder you’ve got a standing therapy thing.

Melissa: Yep, Midtown, Eskenazi – Dr. O’Donnell. Biweekly “burnout check-in.” I guard my Tuesdays at 4 like a dragon.

Lena: Good. Also, if you need to move our 5th-business-day check-in, it’s fine.

Melissa: Might have to. I’ve got to meet with Ellie’s counselor at Westlane about that “anxiety around grades and social media” email she sent early March, and also squeeze in a call with IU Health North about my mom’s follow-up cognitive testing after her November fall.

Lena: That is…a lot. Let me know what I can take off your plate.

Melissa: You just did by taking the mail run :)

Subject: Quick Catch-Up?

Hey Laura,

I hope this note finds you well! I was just thinking back to our emails from May, and how much I appreciated having someone to talk to during that whirlwind of emotions. It really helped to have your perspective as I considered the move to that data privacy role at Methodist Hospital.

Looking back, I think withdrawing my application was the right choice, especially after that informal chat with my supervisor about potential promotions next year. It’s funny how things work out, but I do still feel a bit of that “trapped” sensation from time to time. I guess it’s normal in any long-term role, right?

On a brighter note, I’ve been diving into some new projects at the Department that are keeping things interesting. We’re fine-tuning the workflow for our monthly mailings, making sure everything runs smoothly and securely. I’m really hopeful these improvements will be a step in the right direction.

Anyway, let me know if you’d like to grab a coffee sometime soon. It’d be great to catch up in person and hear what’s new with you too!

Take care,

Melissa

Hi Dan,

I hope your day at North Central is going well, and that the academic team is gearing up for their upcoming meet! Just wanted to give you a heads-up on a few things. I've got a busy week at the office, wrapping up the monthly print-and-mail process (Job Number: IDOH-VR-2025-0162). You know how it goes—making sure those certified birth record corrections, paternity affidavit confirmations, and record amendment notices get printed on secure stock and mailed out correctly. It's crucial they go out without a hitch, especially with the recent workflow updates we've been implementing.

The kids have their typical schedules this week. Ellie can't stop talking about high school marching band, so I’ll be looking into the costs for that soon. Jake has his math tutoring on Tuesday and Thursday as usual. I'll be taking him after work, but I might need you to handle dinner one of those nights if I'm running late. 

Also, remember we're heading up to Mom's in Carmel on Saturday to check in on her. She’s been having more trouble with her arthritis, so I’m thinking it might be time to discuss getting some help with her yard work. Maybe you can bring it up gently when we're there.

Lastly, in case anyone needs to reach me, my contact number at work is (317) 555-2843, and of course, you have my email. Just in case something comes up with the IDOH-047 project or any other last-minute issues.

Looking forward to a quiet Sunday, hopefully, with some time to relax on the porch while Milo gets his exercise. See you tonight!

Love,
Melissa
